name: Havey Tourmaline Quarry
short_description: Historic tourmaline quarry in Maine's mineral-rich Franklin County. Hunt for vibrant tourmaline crystals, quartz, and feldspar in this classic New England rockhounding destination.
description:
Havey Tourmaline Quarry is one of Maine's most productive tourmaline localities, located in the geologically rich Franklin County region known for exceptional pegmatite deposits. The quarry exposes a classic granite pegmatite body that has yielded high-quality tourmaline specimens for over a century. Visitors can search the quarry walls, tailings piles, and surrounding areas for black tourmaline (schorl), occasionally colored varieties, clear quartz crystals, and feldspathic material.

The site sits within Maine's Appalachian bedrock, where Paleozoic-aged granitic intrusions created ideal conditions for pegmatite formation. These coarse-grained igneous rocks crystallized slowly from silica-rich magma, allowing large mineral crystals to develop. Franklin County is particularly famous for tourmaline, having produced some of the finest specimens in North America.

Terrain at the quarry is relatively gentle with light forest cover and open exposures where previous mining activity has left accessible mineral-bearing rock. The site offers both casual surface collecting and more serious specimen hunting. Best results come from examining the pegmatite outcrops, worked areas, and tailings where crystals are frequently exposed.

This is a destination for both beginners and experienced collectors. The quarry's history of professional mining means good material remains accessible without extensive digging. Many visitors enjoy exploring the different faces and pockets that have formed over decades of weathering and occasional disturbance.
history: Havey Tourmaline Quarry developed in the late 1800s and early 1900s as part of Maine's tourmaline mining boom, when gemstone and specimen material from Franklin County pegmatites supplied international mineral markets and jewelry makers. The granite pegmatite body that hosts the tourmaline formed during the Acadian Orogeny approximately 380 million years ago, when the collision of continental plates generated intense heat and pressure that created ideal crystallization conditions for tourmaline and other pegmatite minerals.
tips: Search the exposed pegmatite faces and weathered outcrops where tourmaline crystals are most visible - black tourmaline appears as dark prismatic crystals embedded in white or pinkish feldspar matrix. Use a geology hammer and chisel to carefully extract specimens from the pegmatite rather than breaking indiscriminately; tourmaline is hard but can fracture along crystal planes. Examine the loose talus and tailings piles at the quarry base, where weathering has released individual crystals and specimens that can be collected without excavation. Look for the characteristic triangular cross-section of tourmaline crystals and the glassy luster that distinguishes them from surrounding minerals.
terrain_notes: Elevation 689 feet with gentle slopes throughout the property. Quarry exposes open rock faces and weathered pegmatite outcrops surrounded by light forest cover typical of western Maine. Walking distance from parking to main collecting areas is short, generally under 100 yards. Footing is uneven with loose rock fragments, talus piles, and exposed bedrock creating a moderately rugged surface. No wheelchair access. Sturdy boots recommended for navigating quarry floor and outcrop areas.
best_season: May - October. Late spring through early fall offers the most pleasant conditions for quarry work in Maine. Summer months (June-August) provide warmest temperatures and lowest chance of wet conditions, though quarry can be muddy after rain. Spring (May) and fall (September-October) are ideal for avoiding peak summer heat and tourist crowds. Winter ice and snow accumulation can make quarry access hazardous from November through April.
written_directions: From Strong, take Route 16 north for approximately 1.5 miles. Watch for local directional signs or ask for specific quarry access directions at nearby businesses in Strong, as the exact quarry entrance may be unmarked or seasonally gated. GPS coordinates 44.8333, -70.2203 will bring you to the general location in Franklin County's quarry district. Contact local rockhounding clubs or visitor information for current access details and any landowner protocols.
road_conditions: Paved road access (Route 16) to Strong. Local access roads may be unpaved or rough depending on current quarry conditions. High-clearance vehicle may be beneficial but not required for standard passenger cars.
parking_notes: Parking area varies depending on current quarry operations and landowner conditions. Space typically available for several vehicles near quarry entrance. Ask locally for current parking status and access protocols.
collection_rules: This is private land. Always obtain permission from landowner before collecting. Personal use only, no commercial collection. Respect all posted signs and seasonal closures. Pack out all trash. Do not remove large blocks or cause unnecessary damage to quarry structures. Follow standard rockhounding ethics: take only what you will use, leave the site in good condition for future visitors.
rules:
Obtain landowner permission before collecting
Personal use only - no commercial collection
Respect posted signs and seasonal closures
Pack out all trash
Do not damage quarry infrastructure
Use hand tools only - no heavy equipment
Follow standard rockhounding ethics
nearest_services: Strong, Maine approximately 1-2 miles south with basic services including gas and food options. Phillips, Maine (approximately 6 miles south) offers more comprehensive services including restaurants, stores, and lodging.
accessibility_notes: Relatively easy to moderate terrain suitable for most rockhounders. Uneven quarry floor with loose rock fragments requires attention to footing. Light scrambling may be needed to access some specimen areas. Not wheelchair accessible. Children should be supervised near open quarry faces and piles. Sturdy hiking boots recommended.

faq.question: What tourmaline colors can I find at Havey Tourmaline Quarry in Maine?
faq.answer: Havey Quarry is most famous for black tourmaline (schorl), which appears as dark prismatic crystals embedded in the pegmatite matrix. Occasionally collectors find more valuable colored varieties including green and red tourmaline, though black tourmaline dominates the site. The quarry also yields excellent quartz and feldspar specimens to accompany tourmaline finds.

faq.question: Do I need a permit to rockhound at Havey Tourmaline Quarry?
faq.answer: Havey Tourmaline Quarry is private land, so no permit is needed but landowner permission is required. Contact local rockhounding clubs in Franklin County or visitor information in Strong for current access protocols and permission details. Some operators may allow casual collecting under specific conditions.

faq.question: What tools are best for collecting tourmaline at Maine's Havey Quarry?
faq.answer: A geology hammer and cold chisel work best for carefully extracting tourmaline specimens from the pegmatite. Since tourmaline is hard but can fracture along crystal planes, gentle tapping is more effective than aggressive striking. Many collectors also bring hand tools, gloves, and safety glasses. Loose specimens can often be collected from tailings without any tools.

faq.question: Is Havey Tourmaline Quarry family-friendly and suitable for kids?
faq.answer: Yes, the site is beginner and family-friendly with easy terrain and accessible collecting areas. Children ages 6 and up can participate in collecting from the loose material and accessible outcrop areas. Supervise children around open quarry faces and loose rock piles. The site offers a hands-on introduction to pegmatite minerals and crystal identification.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to visit Havey Tourmaline Quarry for rockhounding?
faq.answer: May through October provides the best conditions for quarry collecting in Maine. Summer months (June-August) offer warmest temperatures and lowest mud, though crowds are largest. Spring and fall deliver ideal weather with fewer visitors. Winter snow and ice from November through April make quarry access hazardous, so avoid this season.

faq.question: How do I identify authentic tourmaline crystals at Havey Quarry?
faq.answer: Look for the characteristic triangular or hexagonal cross-section of tourmaline crystals and their dark black (or occasionally colored) appearance. Tourmaline has a glassy to vitreous luster and forms long prismatic crystals, distinguishing it from surrounding quartz and feldspar minerals. Check the hardness - tourmaline (hardness 7-7.5) will scratch glass and cannot be scratched with a knife, confirming authentic material.

meta_description: Hunt black tourmaline, quartz, and feldspar at Havey Tourmaline Quarry near Strong, Maine. Easy private rockhounding site with excellent Franklin County pegmatite specimens.
